svg.title: Orbit — AI Feature Flow
svg.text: SEQUENCE DIAGRAM — AI FEATURE FLOW (Phase 4 only)

g.text: AiController, AiService, and ClaudeApiClient are Phase 4 only — entirely absent from the build in Phases 1–3. They do not exist as disabled endpoints; they do not exist at all.

svg.text: All five features share the same RateLimitFilter check (20 AI req/min per userId) applied before AiController, and the same graceful error handling:
Claude API failures return a 503 to the user — no bot message is persisted, no Kafka event is published, the conversation is completely unaffected.
FLOW A — /summary COMMAND (broadcast bot message to ALL participants)
3. fetch last {messageLimit} non-deleted messages { senderName, content, createdAt }
build system + user prompt from message list
5. MessageService.insertBotMessage { senderId: BOT_USER_ID, senderName: "Orbit AI", content: summary }
6. publish → chat.messages { botMessage payload, recipientIds: ALL participants }
Summary appears as a bot message from "Orbit AI" — persisted in MongoDB, visible to everyone, indistinguishable from a regular message at the Kafka and WebSocket layer.
FLOW B — @ask MENTION (broadcast bot message to ALL participants)
The @ask message itself is sent via the normal message flow — visible to the group immediately, independent of this pipeline (discussions/012).
1. POST /api/v1/ai/ask { conversationId, question: "What is the difference between WebSockets and SSE?" }
3. fetch last 20 messages (conversation context for Claude)
build prompt: system role + conversation context + user question
4. POST /v1/messages { model, system prompt + context + question, max_tokens: 1000 }
8. KafkaConsumer delivers bot message to ALL participants
FLOW C — SMART REPLY SUGGESTIONS (requester only — no broadcast, no persistence)
Triggered automatically by React SPA after receiving a new inbound message. Background call — does not block the UI.
1. POST /api/v1/ai/suggest-replies { conversationId, messageId } (the message to reply to)
3. fetch target message + last 10 messages as context
build prompt: generate 3 short contextual reply suggestions as JSON array
4. POST /v1/messages { model, prompt requesting JSON array of 3 suggestions, max_tokens: 200 }
[ "Sounds good!", "Can you share more?", "I'll look into it." ]
5. 200 { suggestions: [ "…", "…", "…" ] } → requester ONLY (no Kafka, no bot message, no broadcast)
FLOW D — MESSAGE TRANSLATION (requester only — no broadcast, no persistence)
On-demand: user clicks "Translate" on a specific message. Available on any message in any conversation.
3. fetch message document to get content
build prompt: translate content, detect source language, return JSON
4. POST /v1/messages { model, prompt requesting { translatedContent, detectedLanguage }, max_tokens: 500 }
FLOW E — TONE CHECKER (pre-send — stateless, no DB read, no persistence, no broadcast)
Pre-send: fires when user presses send and tone check is enabled. Fires BEFORE the message is transmitted to the server.
1. POST /api/v1/ai/tone-check { content: "Why haven't you done this yet? It's been three days." }
NO MessageRepository call — tone check is purely stateless on input text
build prompt: analyse tone, flag if aggressive/unclear, suggest softer alternative
3. POST /v1/messages { model, prompt requesting { flagged, reason, suggestion }, max_tokens: 300 }
{ flagged: true, reason: "may read as accusatory", suggestion: "Could you give me an update…" }
4. 200 { flagged, reason, suggestion } → requester ONLY (no Kafka, no bot message, no broadcast)
FLOW F — CLAUDE API ERROR HANDLING (shared by all five features)
1. any AI feature request arrives at AiController
2. AiService constructs prompt and calls ClaudeApiClient
3. POST /v1/messages → Anthropic returns error or times out (30s timeout, 2 retries with backoff)
API error (5xx / rate limit / timeout / overloaded)
5. 503 { message: "AI feature temporarily unavailable. Please try again." }
BROADCAST vs REQUESTER-ONLY AT A GLANCE
